item.title: SSH keys directory created without mode 0700 — key filenames world-listable on multi-user systems
item.intent: The SSH keys directory must not be listable by other users — mkdirSync must set mode 0700 so the directory contents are not exposed even though individual key files are mode 0600.
item.description: src/cloud/provisioning/ssh-keygen.ts line 37 calls mkdirSync(keysDir, { recursive: true }) without a mode option. This creates the keys directory (deployDir/keys/) with default permissions (0755 on most Linux systems), making the directory world-readable. While individual key files are mode 0600 (correct), any user on the system can ls the keys directory to enumerate which instance IDs have keys stored. The fix is to pass { recursive: true, mode: 0o700 } to mkdirSync, matching the same protection principle used for credentials.ts. The same issue exists in registry.ts line 57 for the registry parent directory, but that directory only contains the registry file which is already 0600.
steps: Read src/cloud/provisioning/ssh-keygen.ts lines 34-50 — confirm mkdirSync(keysDir, { recursive: true }) has no mode option
Change mkdirSync call to mkdirSync(keysDir, { recursive: true, mode: 0o700 }) — this ensures the directory is created owner-only even if an existing parent was 0755
Read src/cloud/provisioning/registry.ts writeInstanceRegistry() — confirm mkdirSync there also lacks a mode; add mode: 0o700 to that call as well
Run ssh-keygen.test.ts and registry.test.ts — confirm both pass and no regressions
acceptanceCriteria: The keys directory (deployDir/keys/) is created with mode 0700 — stat confirms no group/other read bits
The registry parent directory is created with mode 0700
Existing key file mode 0600 behavior is unchanged
